text: Introduction of scope revealed stretched diaphragm with convexity towards the abdomen and absence of stomach, spleen and part of colon.
Dissection started from the medial end of posterior lateral defect in the diaphragm.
Momentum was pulled down and the band adherent to the colon was divided.
The objective was early decompression of the stomach and carefully repositioning the viscera back into the peritoneal cavity.
However, a grossly distended stomach and transverse colon could not be delivered through the diaphragmatic defect.
Considering the urgency of early decompression of the stomach, we decided to widen the diaphragmatic defect.
The diaphragm was incised using approximately 2 cm long incision resembling a pendulum arm in relation to bogdallic foramen as shown in the insert.
The incision made our task easy as the antrum of the stomach popped out into the peritoneal cavity and the distended distal half of the stomach was brought down easily into the peritoneal cavity.
A gentle traction was given to the transverse colon and was retracted back into the abdomen through the lateral edge of the diaphragmatic defect.
At this juncture, due to re-rotation of the gastric volvulus, nasogastric tube was easily negotiated.
Stomach was slowly decompressed of air and gastric fluid to avoid pulmonary edema.
Through the diaphragmatic window, minimal pleural fluid collected in the left hemithorax was sucked out with a suction cannula.
Additions between stomach and lung were observed.
Diligent adhesiolysis was achieved using a combination of suction cannula and harmonic scalpel dissection.
A meticulous division of firm and flimsy adhesions finally separated the stomach and the lung without any injury to the lung or the viscera.
The adhesiolysis facilitated retrieval of stomach into the abdomen and exposure of the peritoneal attachments at the superior pole of the spleen.
These were severed and spleen was mobilized off the attachments.
Still, there was a tendency of both spleen and stomach to migrate back into the thoracic territory, probably due to the memory for thoracic domain.
The posterolateral aspect of diaphragmatic defect had only a thin peritoneal fold without any muscle confirming bogdellic hernia.
Transabdominal suturing posed difficulty due to the retractile tendency of the spleen and stomach to pluck the diaphragmatic defect.
Therefore, we decided a thoracoscopic approach for suturing the diaphragmatic defect.
On thoracoscopic view, the spleen and the stomach were seen in the thoracic cavity through the defect, as seen in the inset.
The laparoscopic and thoracoscopic team worked in tandem to retrieve the viscera back to the anatomical location.
Thoracic cavity was inspected for positioning of esophagus and the heart.
While laparoscopic team retracted the stomach and spleen back in the abdomen, primary repair of diaphragm with continuous polypropylene sutures was safely accomplished thoracoscopically.
The sutured diaphragmatic defect measured 6.8 centimeter in dimension.
We reinforced the sutured diaphragmatic defect laparoscopically using a 15 centimeter by 15 centimeter size composite mesh with an adequate overlap with tackers.
Intercostal drainage tube was then inserted and the lung was inflated well without any air leak.
